content:
3 min read
2 hours ago

--

There was a time when documentation was the part of development I dreaded the most.
Build a feature.
Write documentation.
Update API docs.
Modify architecture notes.
Regenerate README.
Someone changes the code…
Update everything again.
Repeat.
It felt like I was spending more time maintaining documentation than actually writing software.
There were days when I would postpone documentation with the classic excuse:
“I’ll update it before the release.”
Spoiler: I didn’t.
Then came the next sprint.
More changes.
More documentation.
More guilt.
Eventually, documentation became outdated, onboarding became slower, code reviews took longer, and developers started asking the same questions repeatedly because the docs no longer reflected the code.

Then I Found JSDoc Scribe
Since I started using JSDoc Scribe, documentation has become something I don’t have to think about constantly.
(Okay… “my life found peace” might be an exaggeration… but only a little. 😄)
Instead of manually maintaining everything, I now generate documentation directly from the source code.
That means whenever I write code with proper JSDoc comments, I can generate:

• API Documentation

• Module Documentation

• Function References

• README sections

• Markdown documents

• Developer-friendly documentation
All from the same source of truth.
No more copy-pasting.
No more forgotten updates.
No more outdated documentation living in some Confluence page nobody remembers to edit.

Documentation Shouldn’t Be a Separate Project
One thing I realized over the years is this:
Documentation fails not because developers don’t want to write it.
It fails because maintaining it manually doesn’t scale.
Every feature change creates documentation work.
Every refactor creates documentation work.
Every new developer expects documentation to be updated.
Eventually, documentation becomes another backlog item.
Automation changes that equation.
When documentation is generated from the code itself, it naturally stays closer to reality.

Why I Like JSDoc Scribe
What impressed me most isn’t just that it generates documentation.
It’s that it fits naturally into the development workflow.
Write code.
Add meaningful JSDoc comments.
Generate documentation.
Done.
Instead of documentation feeling like “extra work,” it becomes a by-product of writing good code.

The Bigger Picture
As projects grow, documentation becomes more valuable — not less.
Good documentation helps with:

• Faster onboarding

• Better code reviews

• Easier maintenance

• Knowledge sharing

• Lower dependency on individual developers

• More confidence during refactoring
The irony is that these are exactly the projects where documentation is hardest to maintain manually.
That’s why automation matters.

Final Thoughts
This post started with a joke about finally finding peace after years of documentation struggles.
But there’s a real lesson behind it.
Documentation isn’t supposed to compete with development.
It should be part of development.
If a tool can reduce repetitive work while keeping documentation accurate, that’s time you get back to solve real engineering problems.
I’ve been using JSDoc Scribe, and it’s made documentation feel significantly less like a chore.
https://www.npmjs.com/package/jsdoc-scribe
If you’ve ever found yourself saying, “I’ll update the docs later,” you might enjoy giving it a try.
Sometimes the best developer tools aren’t the ones that help you write more code.
They’re the ones that help you spend less time doing the things you never wanted to do in the first place.
